coredata.dc:title: The trio of Jatropha curcas fruit seeds: A performance battle of three plant-based inhibitors

coredata.dc:description: Jatropha curcas has been attracted much attention due to its superior biodiesel applications. Three novel bio-based inhibitors arisen from its fruit seed meal (JFM): refluxed extract (RJFME), ultrasound-assisted extract (UJFME) and carbon dots (CDs-JFM) are reported for the first time in retarding the corrosion of cold-rolled steel (CRS) in hydrochloric acid (HCl) medium, achieving the highest inhibition efficiencies of 96.5 %, 93.5 % and 96.2 % at 303 K with the addition of 100 mg L−1, respectively. Their excellent performance depends on the spontaneous formation of protective film on CRS surface by Langmuir adsorption mode, which extremely impedes the diffusion of corrosive particles of Cl−, H3O+ and H2O. This results in a substantial reduction in corrosion current density and an increase in polarization resistance. Three JFM inhibitors exhibit comparable inhibition capacities in weight loss, electrochemical and surface examinations. CDs-JFM stands out as a more superior inhibitor due to its stable and long-lasting performance in highly corrosive HCl solution and over extended reaction time. Remarkably, the fluorescence properties of CDs-JFM act as a probe to elucidate its adsorption mechanism onto CRS surface. This work unveils the potential of JFM as a novel green inhibitor, developing the highly efficient utilization of Jatropha curcas.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Jatropha curcas has been attracted much attention due to its superior biodiesel applications. Three novel bio-based inhibitors arisen from its fruit seed meal (JFM): refluxed extract (RJFME), ultrasound-assisted extract (UJFME) and carbon dots (CDs-JFM) are reported for the first time in retarding the corrosion of cold-rolled steel (CRS) in hydrochloric acid (HCl) medium, achieving the highest inhibition efficiencies of 96.5 %, 93.5 % and 96.2 % at 303 K with the addition of 100 mg L−1, respectively. Their excellent performance depends on the spontaneous formation of protective film on CRS surface by Langmuir adsorption mode, which extremely impedes the diffusion of corrosive particles of Cl−, H3O+ and H2O. This results in a substantial reduction in corrosion current density and an increase in polarization resistance. Three JFM inhibitors exhibit comparable inhibition capacities in weight loss, electrochemical and surface examinations. CDs-JFM stands out as a more superior inhibitor due to its stable and long-lasting performance in highly corrosive HCl solution and over extended reaction time. Remarkably, the fluorescence properties of CDs-JFM act as a probe to elucidate its adsorption mechanism onto CRS surface. This work unveils the potential of JFM as a novel green inhibitor, developing the highly efficient utilization of Jatropha curcas.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Corrosion is a common natural phenomenon, which converts pure metals into their chemically stable substances, like sulfides, oxides or hydroxides. This process not only leads to significant economic losses but also results in catastrophic failure. Statistically, the global cost of corrosion is estimated to be approximately $2.5 trillion, accounting for roughly 3.4 % of global GDP [1]. Cold-rolled steel (CRS) belongs to a low-alloy steel, it lacks inherent corrosion resistance, its production involves in cleaning process. As a prevalent pickling agent, hydrochloric acid (HCl) has a strong ability to dissolve metal oxides at ambient temperature. It causes minimal hydrogen embrittlement, and the generated product of FeCl2 is easily soluble in water. This produces less residue and a high-quality surface finish to the workpieces. However, the oxide leather is characterized by numerous cracks and pores, with the inner layer being relatively loose. When the CRS surface is wetted with HCl medium, chloride ion (Cl−) can pass through these cracks and pores to corrode the CRS matrix. To mitigate this, it is essential to add a corrosion inhibitor to the HCl pickling solution to protect CRS substrate from Cl− attack during the pickling process. Driven by the concepts of green chemistry and sustainable development, exploration of eco-friendly inhibitors has become an unstoppable trend.

Plant is an important natural reservoir of green corrosion inhibitors, with particular attention being drawn to its extracts (PEs) and carbon dots (CDs) within this category. PEs meet the essential requirements for being effective corrosion inhibitors. The molecular structures of active components in PEs contain functional groups such as benzene rings, and polar heteroatoms like oxygen (O), nitrogen (N), and sulfur (S), which contribute by donating their lone pairs of electrons. The presence of π-bonds within these molecules also enables the donation of electrons necessary for interaction with the metal surface, such as the empty 3d-orbitals of iron (Fe) [2]. In addition, PEs exhibit well solubility, form a robust and extensive adsorptive film on metal surface, and are characterized by their low toxicity and cost-effectiveness [3]. These properties make them an environmentally sustainable and highly viable option for corrosion inhibition. From the other side, CDs, a novel carbon-based nanomaterial with a size of <10 nm, has been actively explored since its initial discovery by Xu et al. in 2004 [4]. Owing to the excellent biocompatibility, non-cytotoxicity, well water solubility and ease of modifiability [5], CDs has a broad range of potential applications across diverse fields, with metal corrosion protection being one of them. Besides, the sp2 hybridized carbon framework of CDs provides exceptional structural robustness under extreme conditions, such as high temperatures, strong pH levels and oxidative environments [6]. This establishes a solid foundation for CDs to function as highly effective metal inhibitor.

The preparation method is directly associated with the active ingredients and structural configuration of the corrosion inhibitor. PEs can be prepared using various methods, among which reflux extraction (RE) and ultrasound-assisted extraction (UAE) are two straightforward approaches. In a brief, RE depends on the cycle of heating and condensation, making it well-suited for the efficient extraction of thermally stable compounds. While UAE utilizes the cavitation and mechanical effects of ultrasonic waves, rendering it ideal for the rapid extraction of heat-sensitive components [7]. Regarding the comparison between the RE and UAE methods for the same plant material, Yu Chen et al. [8], Li Qiu et al. [9] and our previous work [7] have provided a comprehensive investigation on rapeseed meal. The findings revealed two key insights: there is a notable divergence in the broad categories of chemical composition between these two extracts, with amino acids being particularly distinct. Secondly, the inhibitor procured through the RE method demonstrated a markedly enhanced inhibition efficacy for steel, relative to that extracted via UAE within the identical corrosive environment. Furthermore, the refluxed extracts showed greater stability and durability in highly corrosive environments, even over extended reaction time. Hydrothermal synthesis (HTS), a primary approach for preparing CDs, which isolates CDs from bulk carbon. It imposes fewer restrictions on the choice of raw materials, a wide variety of plant-based materials are suitable for the synthesis of CDs. Nonetheless, the chemical compositions of plant are diverse and intricate, posing challenges in pinpointing specific ingredients and quantifying their contents in PEs. This complexity also complicates the regulation of the crystal nucleation and growth processes of CDs, potentially causing significant particle agglomeration. Such agglomeration results in CDs with larger dimensions and less desirable topographical features. In short, the RE, UAE and HTS methods each possess distinct advantages and limitations in the preparation of metallic corrosion inhibitors. RE is conductive to extract heat-stable components, yet it encounters challenges when dealing with heat-sensitive materials. UAE boasts highly efficient and excellent yield, albeit at the expense of higher equipment costs. HTS facilitates the synthesis of corrosion inhibitors with unique structures and enhanced stability, but it imposes stringent requirements on equipment and operational precision. In the practical development and preparation of corrosion inhibitors, it is imperative to select the most appropriate method or a combination thereof, based on the specific target inhibitor composition and performance criteria.

Moreover, the structure-activity relationship is intrinsically related to both the inhibition performance and the underlying mechanism. Typically, the inhibitory action of a complete organic compound is contingent upon the adsorption of its active sites, which include aromatic rings and polar heteroatoms within its chemical structures [10]. This principle is exemplified by PEs, which serve as natural product, adhering to the aforementioned mechanism of action. In contrast, the CDs synthesized by HTS method undergoes a series of chemical transformations, including dehydration, carbonization and condensation at elevated temperatures. Fundamentally, the architecture of CDs is predominantly constituted by carbon-based frameworks adorned with various functional groups [11]. Accordingly, CDs has several distinctive characteristics: Firstly, its surface is abundant in O-, N-, and S- functional groups, especially O-rich groups, which can be chemically modified to produce additional adsorption sites, thereby enhancing the inhibitory ability [12]. Secondly, the nano-sized CDs inhibitors enable to self-aggregate by the settlement, forming an organic film on metal surface [13]. In addition, the size of CDs inhibitors (nanometer-scale) is generally larger than that of conventional organic molecules (angstrom-scale), resulting in a protective film that is both thicker and denser [14]. Essentially, the applications of PEs and CDs in corrosion protection primarily relies on the formation of a hydrophobic coating on metallic surface.

Based on the mentioned mechanisms of action, various parts of plants including stems [15], leaves [16], fruits [17] and seeds [18], have garnered significant development in the field of corrosion inhibition. In 1982, Rahman et al. [19] reported on several extracts derived from fenugreek and lupine seeds, as well as from doum, beet and solanum melongena. They subsequently studied the inhibitive effects of these extracts on mild steel, aluminum, zinc, and copper in HCl and sulfuric acid (H2SO4) solutions. Since then, extensive research has been conducted on the anti-corrosion properties of PEs, with a particular focus on their efficacy in protecting steel in 0.5 or 1.0 M HCl environments. Here, several recent representative studies are summarized below. In 2020, Phoenix dactylifera seed extract (PDSE) [20] was used to protect mild steel, presenting an inhibition efficiency (η) of 97.3 % at 2000 mg L−1. The organic film formed by this extract displayed remarkable stability in chronoamperometry test. In 2021, Tan et al. [16] conducted a study on the corrosion-inhibiting capabilities of Betel leaves extract (BLE) on Q235 steel, achieving an η value of approximately 94.0 % at 400 mg L−1. By comparing extraction solvents, they concluded that water offered obvious advantages over organic solvents. In 2022, Fan et al. [21] prepared Passiflora edulia Sims shell extract (PESE), reporting an η value of 95.8 % for mild steel with 500 mg L−1. They also proposed that the chelation between iron oxides and PESE components enhanced the protection efficacy of adsorbed layer. For the same steel and corrosive medium, Rosemary extracts [22] demonstrated an 87 % corrosion inhibition by adding 800 mg L−1. Subsequently, Trifolium repens plant leaves extract [23] reached the maximum η value of 92.1 % for carbon steel corrosion at 1000 mg L−1. By 2025, Tobacco stem extract (TSE) [15] was utilized to inhibit the corrosion of CRS, maintaining an η of 90.2 % at 200 mg L−1. Additionally, extracts derived from Terminalia Catappa leaf [24], Brassica oleracea L [25], Uncaria laevigata [26], Cabbage [27], Moringa Oleifera leaf [28], Acanthopanax senticosus leaf [29], and Fructus cannabis [17] were also developed during this period, all of which exhibited favorable inhibitory effects. However, a common deficiency is that the required dosage of these extracts was relatively high. Therefore, there is a need to explore more efficient plant extracts with superior performance even at lower concentrations.

For the corrosion inhibitors from the same plant source, CDs actually is a simplification and a significant advancement over PEs in terms of the application of chemical constitutions. Consequently, the inhibition performance of plant-derived CDs merits further attention and research. In recent years, a limited number of studies have been reported the function of biomass or plant-derived CDs as corrosion inhibitors. In 2022, Long et al. [30] synthesized a CDs derived from lichi leaves (LCDs) through hydrothermal treatment, which possessed many O- and N-containing functional groups. This LCDs achieved a remarkable η value of 98.1 % for carbon steel in a 1.0 M HCl medium at 200 mg L−1. They still found that the fluorescence intensity and ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) adsorption peaks of this LCDs almost remained unchangeable over a period of two weeks in 1.0 M HCl solution. In the same corrosive medium, two effective CDs inhibitors from Zanthoxylum bungeanum leaves [31] and grapefruit peels [32] were explored in 2023, corresponding impressive η values of 96.0 % and 94.1 %, respectively, at a dosage of 200 mg L−1. In the same year, Xu et al. [33] prepared a CDs inhibitor for copper from mango pulp using HTS method. This work featured several highlights: one was that the CDs attained a η value of 93.0 % in H2SO4 medium with a mere addition of 50 mg L−1. Another was that authors applied the fluorescence properties of CDs to a probe to investigate the inhibition mechanisms by fluorescence spectra (FS) and laser scanning confocal microscope (LSCM) techniques. By 2024, a series of CDs inhibitors derived from pitaya peels [34] and melon seeds [35] were investigated for their efficacy in protecting carbon steel and copper in acidic environments. These inhibitors demonstrated high η values, all exceeding 95.0 %, across varying reaction durations and carbonization temperatures employed in the HTS method. Overall, the development and application of biomass or plant-based CDs as corrosion inhibitors remain insufficient, particularly in terms of in-depth elaboration of its inhibition mechanism through the fluorescent properties of CDs.

Inspired by a wealth of research, oil meal-based raw materials are anticipated to offer protection against the corrosion of CRS in HCl environment. Jatropha curcas, known for its extreme resilience, thrives in rocky desertification areas characterized by severe soil and water scarcity. Remarkably, its seeds exhibit an exceptionally high oil content. Accordingly, the biodiesel produced from fruit seeds of Jatropha curcas has been attracted much attention because of its low S-content and superior quality, making it suitable for various diesel engines. Jatropha curcas fruit seeds meal (JFM), a by-product of the processing. In terms of chemical ingredients, JFM enriches amino acids, organic acids, phenolic and flavonoid compounds [36–39]. This rich chemical profile endows JFM with the potential to serve as a corrosion inhibitor. However, to the best of our knowledge, there is a notable absence of reports on the application of JFM extracts in corrosion inhibition, particularly none concerning the synthesis of CDs-derived inhibitors from JFM.

This work focuses on this by-product of JFM, aiming to reveal its potential as a novel and highly effective corrosion inhibitor at lower dosage. Two PEs (RJFME and UJFME) and one CDs (CDs-JFM) are prepared by RE, UAE and HTS methods, respectively. The active components of these two extracts are analyzed by non-targeted metabolomics. Then, the inhibitive capacities of RJFME and UJFME are predicted via quantum chemical (QC) calculations and molecular dynamics (MD) simulations according to several important information of molecular orbitals, electrostatic potential (ESP), dipole moment (μ), binding energy (E bin), radial distribution function (RDF), fraction free volume (FFV), mean square displacement (MSD) and diffusion coefficient (D). Regarding the characterizations of CDs-JFM, high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD) and atomic force microscope (AFM) are used to determine its crystallographic structure and dimensions. Fluorescence spectra (FS), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy coupled with attenuated total reflection mode (ATR-FTIR) and UV–vis techniques are applied to observe its optical properties and organic groups. Elemental analysis is completed by energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) coupled with HR-TEM and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) techniques. The stability of CDs-JFM solution is also evaluated by zeta potential (E zeta). After proving the successful preparation of CDs-JFM, the inhibitory effects of these three inhibitors on CRS in 1.0 M HCl medium are fully studied by weight loss experiments and electrochemical analyses. Surface roughness and hydrophilicity of the corroded and inhibited CRS samples are compared through AFM, Laser microscope (LM), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), metallographic microscopy (MM) and water contact angle (WCA). The adsorbed species on CRS surface are detected by EDS matched with SEM, XPS and flight-secondary ion mass spectrometry (TOF-SIMS). In order to further excavate and utilize the excellent optical and fluorescence properties of the CDs, FS is used to exam the concentration change of the CDs-JFM solution before and after inhibition, LSCM is employed to observe the real-time adsorption of CDs-JFM onto CRS surface, further enriching the application prospects of the CDs-JFM. Collectively, these findings provide both theoretical and experimental evidences for the application of JFM as a novel and highly efficient plant-derived corrosion inhibitor.

Cold-rolled steel (CRS) was purchased from a local steel supplier, with its composition primarily consisting of Fe, along with trace amounts (wt%) of C (0.006), S (0.001), P (0.013), Al (0.048) and Mn (0.210). Prior to the experiment, CRS sample must be abraded using a series of waterproof sandpapers (60 #, 120 #, 300 #, 600 #, 1200 #, 1500 #, 2000 #), then cleaned with acetone and cut to a uniform size of 20 × 25 × 0.75 mm. The steel working electrode consisted of a piece of steel (1 × 1 × 1 cm3) sealed with a mixture of epoxy resin and polyamides (2:1 by weight), and one side was exposed to the corrosive solution for examinations. 5.0 M HCl stock solution was prepared from concentrated HCl (AR, 36–38 wt%), and then diluted to the working solution (1.0 M) in deionized water (< 1.0 μS cm−1, produced by a water purifier). 1.0 g L−1 of three JFM inhibitors was dissolved from an individual solid, presenting light yellow in color and clear, but the CDs-JFM has best solubility and clearest solution. Experimental levels (10–100 mg L−1) of these inhibitors were dispersed into the 1.0 M blank HCl solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and structural analysis of three JFM inhibitors
ce:section.ce:para: In order to fully exploit the corrosion inhibition potential of JFM, three routes in Fig. 1(a) are selected to obtain JFM inhibitors, including rout I of RE, rout II of UAE, and rout III of HTS. Regarding extraction, 10 g of JFM powder was immersed in 40 % (volume fraction) C2H5OH for 2 h at room temperature. RE was conducted for 3 h at 70 °C in a constant temperature water bath. UAE was performed for 40 min at 40 Hz, 40 °C and 100 % power. Subsequent procedure was the same, i.e. the supernatant after filtration was evaporated to remove C2H5OH, then two brown solids (RJFME and UJFME) were remained after hot air-drying at 50 °C. HTS was carried out utilizing a high-pressure reactor, taking a dispersed mixture of 2 g JFM powder and 60 mL of deionized water. After reacting for 12 h at 180 °C and cooling to ambient temperature, the upper solution was passed through a 0.22 μm membrane. Pre-freezing for 24 h, and then freeze-drying to obtain CDs-JFM as a brown and loose solid powder.

The chemical constituents of two JFM extracts were analyzed through non-targeted metabolomics (LC-30A Triple TOF 6600 +). Four representative compounds of apigenin, uracil, syringaldehyde, aspartic acid (Asp) (Fig. 1(c)), and their protonated structures (p-) were selected for theoretical investigation based on density functional theory (DFT) by Materials studio software (Version 2017), aiming to predict the inhibitory potentials of these extracts. The site most susceptible to protonation for a molecule corresponds to the greatest protonated affinity energy (PAE) via calculation [40]. QC calculation was unfolded with BLYP-GGA level and DND basis set, to obtain the energies of the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO), the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) and their energy gap ΔE gap (ΔE gap = E LUMO - E HOMO), and ESP. MD simulations were applied to simulate the adsorptive configuration of these molecules on the Fe (1 1 0) plane. E bin and RDF were obtained in a box containing 500 of H2O, 1 of molecule, 5 of H3O+, 5 of Cl− and Fe slab. NVT ensemble, 1 fs time step, 1000 ps total simulation time, and COMPASS forcefield were set up, temperature was controlled at 298 K. In addition, the diffusion behavior of inhibitor molecules and corrosive particles (Cl−, H3O+ and H2O) was compared with and without JFM inhibitors. The inhibited box contained 100 of molecules, 5 of H2O, 5 of H3O+ and 5 of Cl−, while the blank box composed of 500 of H2O, 5 of H3O+ and 5 of Cl−, which was used for FFV, MSD and D, other setting was same as in the previous simulation.

To validate the successful preparation of CDs-JFM, the HR-TEM (Talos F200S), XRD (Rigaku ULTIMAIV) and AFM (Bruker Dimension Icon) and were used to observe its crystallographic structure and dimensions. FS (F-7100), ATR-FTIR (Bruker TENSOR 27) and UV–vis (2800 A) were applied to determine its optical properties and organic groups. Elemental analysis was achieved by EDS coupled with HR-TEM and XPS (K-Alpha+) techniques. LSCM (Leica STELLARIS 5) was employed to observe the fluorescence of CDs-JFM aqueous solution. The stability of CDs-JFM solution was also accessed by E zeta.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Weight loss and CRS surface analysis
ce:section.ce:para:
The abraded CRS pieces were precisely weighted and fully immersed in 1.0 M HCl solution, both blank and containing different concentrations of these three JFM inhibitors. After reacting for 6 h at controlled temperatures (293–323 K) maintained by a water bath, CRS samples were removed, cleaned, dried and reweighed. Three parallel experiments were conducted to obtain reliable data. The corrosion rate (v) and inhibition efficiency (η w) were obtained [41].

Here, w 1 and w 2 (g) are the weights of CRS specimen before and after reaction, S (m2) refers to the total surface area of CRS sheet, and t (h) is immersion time. v 0 and v (g m−2 h−1) correspond to the values of corroded CRS, and the CRS samples inhibited by JFM inhibitors, respectively. Based on the weight loss experiments, the adsorption of JFM inhibitors was evidenced using spectroscopy techniques of ATR-FTIR, UV–vis and FS. Meanwhile, the inhibitory effects of JFM inhibitors also can be visualized by the changes of surface roughness and hydrophilicity/hydrophobicity of CRS samples before and after inhibition. These surface characteristics can be detected by AFM, LM (KEYENCE VK-X150), SEM (Zeiss Sigma 300) attached with EDS (Bruker Quantax XFlash SDD 6│30), MM (10XB-PC) and WCA (JC2000C1). Furthermore, XPS and TOF-SIMS (TOF.SIMS 5–100) were employed to detect the elements and their groups on inhibited CRS surface. LSCM to observe the adsorption of CDs-JFM onto CRS surface, further confirming the absorption mechanism of JFM inhibitors.

A three-electrodes system of an electrochemical workstation (PARSTAT 2273) was applied to carry out electrochemical tests at 303 K, which composed of a steel working electrode (WE), a reference electrode of saturated calomel electrode (SCE) and a platinum counter electrode (PCE). Open circuit potential reached stable after immersing for 1800 s, and then its value (E OCP) was recorded. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) experiments were performed over a sine wave with frequencies from 105 to 10−2 Hz and an amplitude of 10 mV. Potentiodynamic polarization (PDP) curves were conducted at a scan rate of 0.5 mV s−1 over a potential range of −250 mV to 250 mV (vs. E OCP). Inhibition efficiency from EIS (η R) and PDP curves (η P) are calculated [42].

Here, the subscript of (inh) and (0) correspond to the values with and without JFM inhibitors. R p and i are the polarization resistance and corrosion current density, respectively. The potential of zero charge (E PZC) was obtained by EIS tests at different bias electrode potentials (E).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Compositional analysis and activity prediction of two JFM extracts
ce:section.ce:para: As illustrated in the annular diagram (Fig. 1(b)), the classification of chemical compositions of RJFME and UJFME exhibit remarkable similar. Among them, amino acids constitute the most abundant group, followed by organic acids and aromatic compounds. Additionally, alkaloids, phenolic acids, heterocyclic compounds and flavonoids are also present in notable proportions. Evidently, these chemical constituents are rich in electron-donating groups, such as O, N polar heteroatoms, CC and benzene ring. These functional sites endow two JFM extracts with adsorptive properties, enabling them to interact with steel surface to form protective barriers.

“High HOMO but low LUMO with a small energy gap” is a widely accepted criterion for screening effective corrosion inhibitors. As shown in Fig. 2 , apart from the comparable values of apigenin and p-apigenin, the HOMO and LUMO values of other compounds exhibit a significant decline following protonation. This trend indicates that neutral compounds have limited electron beam capacity and are more likely to readily loss electrons. In contrast, the lower LUMO values, which are closer to the Fermi level of Fe (−5.177 eV), making their protonated molecules are more susceptible to accept electrons from the steel surface, thereby facilitating the formation of adsorption layer [43]. Meanwhile, the ΔE gap (eV) values are ordered as follows: p-syringaldehyde (1.762) < p-apigenin (2.158) < apigenin (2.816) < p-Asp (2.917) < p-uracil (3.045) < syringaldehyde (3.203) < uracil (3.900) < Asp (4.401). Notably, the ΔE gap values of the protonated structures are consistently lower than those of their neutral molecules, highlighting the pivotal role of protonation in enhancing corrosion inhibition efficacy. This conclusion is further supported by the analysis of molecular dipole moment (μ). In general, compounds with higher μ value typically perform stronger anti-corrosion ability due to their increased interaction with metal surface. As illustrated in Fig. 2, with only a minor decrease for p-apigenin, the μ values of protonated structures are improved relative to their neutral counterparts. This suggests that protonation enhances the adsorption capacity and inhibition performance of these compounds. Interestingly, the protonation occurs predominantly at CO group, which has the maximum PAE value among all studied components. This can be attributed to a fact that the electron-withdrawing effect of the Cδ+ centra renders O atom extremely electron-deficient, and thus making it highly susceptible to attracting H3O+ in acidic solution. In addition, ESP is used to access to the local activity of a molecule. In Fig. 2, relatively negative and positive region are labeled in red and blue respectively. Overall, the negative areas are clearly defined and are primarily associated with O atoms from functional groups such as CO, OH, COC, and benzene ring. These regions act as the centers for electrophilic reaction, where they donate their electrons to the empty 3d-orbitals of Fe atom, thereby forming covalent bonds. Conversely, a few positive regions are observed on the saturated CH structures, indicating potential sites for nucleophilic interactions. Focusing on the protonated sites, there is a remarkable decrease in electronegativity, with a gradual shift towards positive centers. Regarding the N atoms, whether protonated or not, it always serves as positive charge characteristics in uracil, while displaying a weak negativity in Asp. This suggests that the N atoms can not only receive electrons from steel surface to form covalent bonds, but also donate electrons to acquire a certain positive charge. This positive charge enables them to attract Cl−, thus inducing physisorption.

For an adsorptive-type corrosion inhibitor, the adsorption and film-forming behavior on metal surface are two critical indicators that can be evaluated by several important parameters, including binding energy (E bin), radial distribution function (RDF), mean square displacement (MSD) and diffusion coefficient (D). As can be seen in Fig. 3(a), all the studied compounds are capable of stably adsorbing onto Fe (1 1 0) surface in a parallel orientation throughout the simulation period. Their E bin values remain relatively consistent before and after protonation. Specifically, apigenin and p-apigenin have higher E bin, indicating that these compounds should contribute more effectively to corrosion retardation. The adsorptive type of an inhibitor can be determined by the RDF. In this study, the distance r is defined as the separation between the O, N atoms, benzene ring and Fe atom. As shown in Fig. 3(b), the r values for OFe, NFe and benzene ring-Fe interactions are almost below 3.5 Å. This implies that these sites and groups can interact with Fe surface by chemisorption, i.e. forming covalent bonds with the free electrons of Fe atom [44]. In contrast, the r values for NFe in Asp and p-Asp are around 4.5 Å, meaning that these two sites are prone to engage in physisorption. This type of interaction can hinder the movement of Cl− towards steel surface.

FFV is defined as the proportion of free volume (V f) to total volume (V t) in a system (FFV = V f / V t) [18]. Herein, V f corresponds to the entire space that is not occupied by inhibitor molecules and is available for the motion of other molecules and particles. Thus, a lower FFV value indicates the formation of a denser film by the corresponding inhibitor molecule. This denser film more powerfully restricts the movement of corrosive particle, thereby enhancing inhibition efficiency [45]. In the target box depicted in Fig. 4(a), the grey areas represent the occupied spaces, while the purple zones indicate the voids. Overall, the grey placements dominate in all studied structures. It is concluded that the substantial organic compounds existing in both RJFME inhibitors can aggregate to form a protective film, thus preventing the migration of corrosive particles of H2O, H3O+ and Cl− to steel surface. In detail, the FFV values of four neutral molecules follow the order: syringaldehyde < Asp < uracil < apigenin, with the same turn observed for their protonated counterparts. This behavior likely stems from their inherent chemical structural characteristics. Notably, the FFV value of each protonated structure is smaller than that of its neutral molecule. This can be attributed to protonation enhancing intermolecular forces, thereby promoting the formation of a denser inhibitory layer. In addition, the film-forming process can be tracked by MSD. Given the relationship between MSD and D, the D value can be obtained from the slope of MSD curve [46].
(5)
D =
1 6
lim
t → ∞
dMSD dt
Here, a same time interval of 200–800 ps is intercepted to fit the MSD curves and calculate the slope. In Fig. 4(b) and (c), compared with the pure aqueous phase, the MSD curves of H2O, H3O+ and Cl− in different inhibitor membranes become flat after adding JFM extracts. This is accompanied by a dramatic decrease in D values, especially for H3O+ and Cl−. These further confirm that the layers formed by JFM extracts not only replace neutral H2O molecule, but also efficiently block the movement of charged H3O+ and Cl−. This dual action is crucial in achieving the goal of corrosion inhibition. Furthermore, the self-diffusion behavior of inhibitor molecules can reflect the stability of the protective film. As illustrated in the histogram in Fig. 4(b), the self-D values of neutral compounds are generally lower than those of their protonated structures, with the exception of Asp. This shows that the molecules become more mobile and active after protonation. Meanwhile, apigenin meets the smallest self-D, pointing that its film should attach stably to steel surface. This stability is likely due to the large spatial resistance of its chemical structure, which makes it less prone to relocation. In conclusion, according to the results of QC calculations and MD simulations, the presence of a large number of π-electron sites distributed CO, CC and O, N heteroatoms significantly enhances the interaction between the organic compounds and steel surface. This interaction occurs through both chemisorption and physisorption, demonstrating the corrosion inhibition capabilities of these two JFM extracts.

While superior inhibition performance is the primary consideration, it is equally important to ensure that the CDs inhibitor possesses the general and typical characteristics of CDs, such as morphology, lattice structure and grain size. Therefore, a range of techniques shown in Fig. 5 are employed to confirm whether the CDs-JFM has been successfully prepared. TEM is a traditional tool for studying the microstructure and morphology of CDs materials. In Fig. 5(a), HR-TEM reveals many clear lattice fringes within the carbon cores, with a lattice spacing of 0.34–0.36 nm. This is consistent with the lattice constant of C (002) plane [47]. Moreover, these lattice fringes appear as more conspicuous carbon honeycomb arrays at a scale of 5 nm, further indicating that the CDs-JFM is high crystalline. AFM is used to quantify the size of nanoparticles found in CDs-JFM, Fig. 5(b) is an AFM height image, showing a uniform distribution of particles with heights ranging from approximately 2 to 8 nm. Besides, fluorescence serves as a characteristic feature of CDs. Fig. 5(c) displays the LSCM map of 100 mg L−1 CDs-JFM solution in 1.0 M HCl solution, Despite the fact that water and acid typically quench fluorescence, a distinct fluorescence signal is still evident. This not only confirms the appearance of nanoparticles, but also lays a strong basis for the adsorption of CDs-JFM onto CRS surface in acidic corrosive solutions. Fig. 5(d) drafts the FS curves of 100 mg L−1 CDs-JFM at excitation wavelengths ranging from 320 to 440 nm. As shown, the maximum fluorescence wavelength is red-shifted from 403 nm to approximately 505 nm, which is a unique fluorescence property of CDs. The XRD spectra of CDs-JFM powder is depicted in Fig. 5(e). The broad peak at ∼14° arises from disordered C structures, whereas the ∼22° diffraction feature corresponds to the graphitic C (0 0 2) plane [48]. E zeta is a key indicator of the stability of CDs solution and the possibility for CDs to agglomeration or precipitation. As shown in Fig. 5(e), E zeta value of −22.9 mV, which is comparable to that of universal CDs materials. This indicates that the CDs-JFM is sufficiently stable in aqueous solution, minimizing the risk of aggregation and precipitation [30]. This negative E zeta value is associated with the presence of O-containing groups on the surface of CDs-JFM, for example OH and COOH. These groups can release H+ in aqueous solution, making CDs-JFM surface negatively charged [11]. Do the polar atoms such as O, N and even S exist on CDs-JFM surface? ATR-FTIR, UV–vis and elemental analyses provide compelling evidence. As seen from Fig. 5(f) and (g), 100 mg L−1 CDs-JFM solution exhibits a blue color under 365 nm UV light, which is not visible under daylight condition. The ATR-FTIR spectrum provides detailed insights into the functional groups present: a wide peak ranging from 3620 to 3220 cm−1 responds to the stretching vibration of OH or NH group. A peak at 2070 cm−1 is classified as the extensible vibration of CC bond [49]. 1635 cm−1 is a characteristic peak of CC/CN/CO [49]. 662 cm−1 is the out-of-plane bending vibration of ≡CH/CH (ArH) [16]. Meanwhile, the UV–vis adsorption peak at 203 nm, 228 and 283 nm are attributed to π-π* transitions, corresponding to aromatic domains and CC sp2 domains. The peak at 320 nm is related to n-π* jump of CN or CO [5]. These observations confirm the presence of unsaturated structures and O, N heteroatoms in CDs-JFM. In addition, the presence of C, O, N, S elements are confirmed through EDS line-scanning analysis in conjunction with HR-TEM. As shown in Fig. 5(h1), the atomic fractions of N (0.11 %) and S (0.03 %) are relatively low, inferring that the adsorption behavior of CDs-JFM predominantly governed by O atoms and other unsaturated C-structures. Moreover, XPS analysis in Fig. 5(h2) reveals the existence of significant quantities of C, O, N, along found with trace amounts of S and their related functional groups. In C 1 s spectrum, three common peaks are found: CC/CH at 284.8 eV, CO/CN at 286.4 eV, and CO/OCO at 288.6 eV [50]. Noteworthy, two unusual peaks of 292.6 and 295.4 eV can be assigned as conjugated C structures, specifically indicating the appearance of aromatic rings or extended π-conjugated system in CDs-JFM [51,52]. In O 1 s, two peaks are fitted at 531.2 and 532.3 eV, which are related to CO and CO/OH, respectively [42]. The N 1 s includes two peaks of NH and CN at 399.7 and 401.5 eV, respectively [53]. Regarding the S 2p, four characteristic peaks are identified: RSR' (161.5 eV), SC (163.6 eV), SO (166.3 eV) and oxidized S species (168.3 eV), corresponding to the various S-containing groups [33]. Collectively, these analytical findings provide conclusive supports for the successful preparation of the CDs-JFM. The identified presence of C, O, N and S elements, coupled with their associated functional groups or residue compounds, strongly indicates that the CDs-JFM has the significant capacity to adsorb onto steel surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Weight loss and spectral characterization experiments
ce:section.ce:para:
As seen in Fig. 6(a), in pure corrosive HCl solution without any inhibitor (c = 0), the corrosion rate grows significantly with the temperature ranging from 293 to 323 K, indicating that the higher temperatures accelerate the corrosion reaction. Consequently, the η w values of RJFME and UJFME generally follows the trend: 293 K > 303 K > 313 K > 323 K at low concentrations, suggesting that a low inhibitor dosage cannot effectively suppress highly aggressive corrosion. At higher concentrations, the inhibitors possess stronger inhibition capabilities, achieving high and comparable η w values, though without a uniform trend. Based on the structure-activity relationship of inhibitors. RJFME at 100 mg L−1 exhibits superior inhibition performance at elevated temperatures (313−323K), likely due to its more heat-stable active components extracted by the reflux method (70 °C). The components in UJFME appear to have slightly lower thermal stability, leading to diminished inhibition performance at 323 K (89.1 %). For CDs-JFM, close η w values are obtained across 293–323 K at dosage of 40–100 mg L−1. This can be attributed to the robust carbon framework of CDs-JFM, which maintains structural stability under varying environmental conditions. Notably, CDs-JFM performs more exceptional effect under 303 K at each tested concentration, suggests this temperature is particularly favorable for maximizing its inhibitory capacity. Focused on 303 K, all three inhibitors demonstrate satisfactory corrosion inhibition performance: η w are up to notable values of 91.5 %, 90.8 % and 90.9 % in the presences of 50 mg L−1 RJFME, 70 mg L−1 UJFME and 30 mg L−1 CDs-JFM, respectively. Especially for CDs-JFM, an impressive η w of 84.5 % is reached with a minimal concentration of merely 10 mg L−1. Even at an elevated temperature of 323 K, its η w still remains 82.6 % at a concentration of only 40 mg L−1. Based on these information, CDs-JFM demonstrates superior performance as an inhibitor compared to the others. In order to investigate the adsorption behavior of these JFM inhibitors, different adsorption isothermal models are applied to fit the weight loss data, but only Langmuir model has a better suitability [54].
(6)
c θ
= c +
1
K ads
Here, c is the concentration (mg L−1) of inhibitor, θ = η
w /100, expresses surface coverage. K ads refers to the adsorption equilibrium constant, which reflects the adsorptive intensity on binding affinity of an inhibitor. As shown in Fig. 6(b), the linear correlation is stronger at 293–303 K. However, it slightly weakens at 313 K and becomes notably poor at 323 K. Another interesting phenomenon is observed for RJFME and UJFME, where the line of c/θ-c appear two obvious sections at 323 K. Nevertheless, the data still conform to Langmuir model, indicating that these three JMF inhibitors undergo homogeneous single-molecule adsorption.

The pickling ability of HCl is directly proportional to its concentration. Therefore, to ensure compatibility with extreme operational conditions, it is essential to research the resistance of corrosion inhibitor at a specific dosage in highly concentrated and aggressive HCl environments. In Fig. 6(c), when the HCl concentration increases from 1.0 to 3.0 M, the lnv values in the existence of 100 mg L−1 JFM inhibitor are significantly lower than those in blank HCl medium. This suggests that three inhibitors possess strong ability to resist highly corrosive HCl. However, the η w of three inhibitors still declines, which implies that their capacity to mitigate the severe attack of Cl− ions is limited at a dose of 100 mg L−1. Furthermore, the extent of decrease in η w exhibits the order of CDs-JFM < UJFME < RJFME, indicating that the CDs-JFM has superior corrosion resistance compared to the other two inhibitors. Immersion time (t) is a key to explore the stable and sustainable effectiveness of a corrosion inhibitor. As illustrated in Fig. 6(d1), the v of CRS in inhibited solution (100 mg L−1) is dramatically reduced at various time intervals compared to that in pure HCl. Regarding the inhibitory effect, the v in corrosive solution keeps growing from 6 h to 24 h, telling the HCl concentration is sufficient to sustain the corrosion of CRS within this period. The corresponding η w values really reflect the inhibitory capacities of three inhibitors. Beyond 24 h, the v of CRS sample in blank HCl solution decreases obviously, implying that the HCl is nearly exhausted. This depletion can be due to three factors: the continuous corrosion reaction, the interaction with JFM inhibitors, and natural evaporation at 303 K. Hence, the high η w values observed between 48 and 72 h are more reasonably explained by the strong stability of the pre-formed adsorptive film. This finding conforms that these three inhibitors exhibit highly stable and durable adsorption performance. Furthermore, the variation in weight over reaction time can be closely linked to the corrosion kinetics. The fitted curves of log(w 1/w 2)-t illustrate the relationship between weight change and corrosion progression [55].
(7)
log
w 1
w 2
=
k 1
2.303
t
Here, w 1 and w 2 share the same physical definition as in Eq. (1). k 1 is the first-order reaction rate constant, which reflects the activity of reactant species. A higher value of k 1 represents more active reactants. As shown in Fig. 6(d2), It is clear that these curves have good linearity with correlation coefficients approaching 1. This reveals the corrosion reaction of CRS is consistent with a first-order reaction, both in the absence and presence of JFM inhibitors [56]. Besides, it means that the addition of JFM inhibitors does not alter the underlying corrosion mechanism of CRS. In terms of k 1, it obeys the order: blank > CDs-JFM > RJFME > UJFME. This means that the addition of JFM inhibitors passivates the reaction species and increases the activation energy of reaction, making the corrosion reaction more difficult to proceed. Moreover, the half-life (t 1/2) of chemical reactants in both corroded and inhibited reactions is obtained by the relationship of t 1/2 = ln2/k 1 [56]. The lowest t 1/2 is encountered by the purely corrosive system, indicating that the aggressive local corrosion induced by Cl− on CRS surface leads to rapid consumption of the reactants. In contrast, the t 1/2 values are apparently prolonged after adding JFM inhibitors, following the order of UJFME > RJFME > CDs-JFM. Combining the above two perspectives, UJFME is considered to be a more stable and sustainable inhibitor.

Spectroscope techniques are instrumental in elucidating the adsorption of corrosion inhibitor. As seen in Fig. 6(e1), the ATR-FTIR spectra of two JFM extracts are more diverse than those of CDs-JFM. Herein, the vibrations such as 2927 cm−1, 1406 cm−1, 1278 cm−1 and 1052 cm−1 are assigned to aliphatic CH stretching, conjugated CC in aromatic ring (Ar), and CO bonds in alcohols and esters, respectively. But, the characteristic CC stretching vibration at 2070 cm−1 is not found. On the inhibited CRS surface, several significant spectral features persist, including CC/CN/CO stretching vibration observed at 1635 cm−1 and the≡CH/CH bending mode at 662 cm−1. The observed shift of the NH/OH from 3408 to 3235 cm−1 is likely attributable to the formation of OFe and NFe bonds. This will be further validated through subsequent XPS and TOF-SIMS analyses. UV–vis spectra illustrated in Fig. 6(e2), exhibit comparable characteristics, revealing consistent π-π* transitions at 203 nm, 228 and 283 nm. These transitions are associated with aromatic domains and CC sp2 domains [5], further confirming the presence of conjugated π-electron systems in these inhibitors. The CDs-JFM spectrum appears an n-π* jump corresponding to CN/CO structure at 320 nm, but lacks the minor transition peak observed at 272 nm. Above observations highlight the shared characteristics between the two JFM extracts while underscoring their distinction from CDs-JFM. However, no consistent pattern is observed in the changes of UV intensity before and after inhibition, suggesting that the different interactions occur between JFM inhibitors and CRS surface. Moreover, the variation of FS intensity of also reflect the adsorption behavior of CDs-JFM. As shown in Fig. 6(e3), at a certain excitation wavelength of 350 nm, the fluorescence intensity exhibits a direct proportionality to the concentration of CDs-JFM. In particular, the intensity of 100 mg L−1 CDs-JFM is remarkably strong, whereas it sharply decreases in 1.0 M HCl solution, inferring that the fluorescent property of CDs-JFM is diminished dramatically in an acidic environment. This may account for the decreases in η w of CDs-JFM with increasing HCl concentration, as depicted in Fig. 6(c). In addition, the fluorescence intensity of CDs-JFM almost remains virtually unchanged after interacting with CRS. This observation implies that the fluorescent characteristic of CDs-JFM is sufficiently strong and stable, which has been minimal influent by the adsorption process between inhibited solution and CRS surface.

Electrochemical corrosion tests play an important role to evaluate and reveal the corrosion process. Fig. 7 (a1) gives the E OCP-t plots of three JFM inhibitors with different additives. It is obvious that the corrosion of CRS and the adsorption of JFM inhibitors achieve a stable state within 1800 s, with fluctuations in their E OCP values remaining below 20 mV. This also signifies that these JFM inhibitors likely exhibit good electrochemical corrosion inhibition performance. As observed in Fig. 7(a2-a4), this similar characteristic of PDP plots indicates that the corrosion protection behavior of JFM inhibitors is related to the generation of a passivation film on steel electrode surface [57]. In Fig. 7(c1), the aggressive attack of Cl− combined with high hydrogen evolution rate at cathode induces severe non-uniform corrosion on steel electrode surface in 1.0 M blank HCl solution, resulting in the highest corrosion current density i corr (884 μA cm−2). With the progressive adsorption of JFM inhibitors in the inhibited system, the steel surface becomes smoother, accompanied by a reduction in i corr values. In comparison, UJFME has the least negative migration in i corr, which leads to its poorer η P values. However, the anodic reaction is complex, with its branches presenting distinct partitions based on the corrosion potential E corr and its gap ΔE corr. These parts consist of an activation-passivation transition zone (I) at ΔE corr1, a passivation zone (II) at ΔE corr2 and a desorption zone (III) over the desorption potential E des. This observation is more pronounced at an inhibitor concentration of 100 mg L−1. For RJFME and CDs-JFM, their zone I is evident, characterized by a greater ΔE corr1 value, suggesting that a significant competition between the corrosion of steel and the inhibitive action of corresponding JFM inhibitors. Once this transition is complete, the steel surface becomes progressively covered by these JFM inhibitors, thereby entering Zone II. It is important to recognize that passivation does not imply the complete cessation of corrosion, rather, it signifies a reduction in the corrosion rate due to the adsorption of inhibitor molecules. Nonetheless, the passivation layer is not imperishable and will gradually dissolve as alterations occur in the pH and ion concentration of the electrolyte solution. Here, the E des values of three JFM inhibitors are around −0.25 V. but the desorption of CDs-JFM occurs only at a low addition of 10 mg L−1, underscoring the stable and enduring adsorption of CDs-JFM at higher concentrations ranging from 50 to 100 mg L−1. This observation appears to align with the influence of HCl dosage and immersion time as depicted in Fig. 6(c) and Fig. 6 (d1). For UJFME, the boundary between zone I and zone II is less clear, which shows that the competitive process of corrosion and inhibition persist over a prolonged duration. This indicates that the electrochemical corrosion inhibition effect of UJFME is comparatively weaker.

As for the E corr drawn in Fig. 7 (c2), the inhibited systems generally move towards more negative potentials relative to the blank corrosive solution, with all values deviating by <85 mV. This indicates that the three JFM inhibitors function as mixed-type corrosion inhibitors affecting both the anodic and cathodic reactions [49]. Tafel slopes of anode (β a) and cathode (β b) are applied to describe the polarization behavior of the respective electrodes. These parameters are indicative of the ease with which polarization occurs and the sensitivity of the reaction rate to potential variations. Specifically, a higher βa means that a more substantial shift in anodic potential is necessary to affect a significant alteration in anodic i corr, indicating that the anodic reaction is more difficult to carry out, and thus the anode exhibits greater stability. On the contrary, a lower βc value denotes that even a small adjustment in cathodic potential can cause a considerable change in cathodic i corr, implying that the cathodic reaction proceeds with greater facility. As a result, in the context of metal corrosion protection, a corrosion inhibitor with a higher βa and βc will contribute more to decelerating the corrosion reaction. As shown in Table S1, the nearly parallel configurations of all cathodic branches result in relatively similar βc values, whereas the significant disparity in βa values is due to separations generated in the anodic regions. With the addition of 100 mg L−1, as well as other lower levels, the βa values of RJFME and CDs-JFM are greater, suggesting that these two inhibitors are more excellent. This view is further supported by the higher η P values associated with these two inhibitors across all tested concentrations. Furthermore, according to the previous report [58], the efficacy of a corrosion inhibitor can be elucidated by two action coefficients pertaining to the anodic reaction (f a) and the cathodic reaction (f c), which serve to explain the corrosion/inhibition mechanism.

Here, the superscript of (inh) and (0) stand for the corrosion reaction occurring in the presence and absence of JFM inhibitors, respectively. Usually, for a given inhibitor, the values of f a and f c are different differ at identical additive concentrations. A diminished value f a either f c causes higher inhibition efficiency [59]. In Table S1, both f a and f c values are <1, indicating that the active sites on CRS surface are covered by JFM inhibitors, which can be due to the geometric locking effect [58]. Besides, the f a or f c values of RJFME and CDs-JFM are lower than those of UJFME at a concentration of 100 mg L−1, corresponding to RJFME and CDs-JFM performing the better electrochemical corrosion inhibition. In addition, both f a and f c decrease with increasing doses of the same JFM inhibitor, accompanied by higher η P values. This further implies that these three JFM inhibitors can be regarded as hybrid inhibitors to resist both anodic and cathodic corrosion reactions simultaneously. Moreover, f a/f
c ratio provides a more intuitive measure to access the relative contribution of anode or cathode inhibition. An intriguing observation from Fig. 7 (c2) is that f a/f c < 1 or very close to 1 at 100 mg L−1 for all three inhibitors, whereas f a/f
c values approach or even exceed 1 at lower levels. This suggests that three JFM inhibitors predominantly tend to exhibit anodic inhibition at higher concentrations but contribute more significantly to cathodic inhibition at lower dosages.

As for the E corr drawn in Fig. 7 (c2), the inhibited systems generally move towards more negative potentials relative to the blank corrosive solution, with all values deviating by <85 mV. This indicates that the three JFM inhibitors function as mixed-type corrosion inhibitors affecting both the anodic and cathodic reactions [49]. Tafel slopes of anode (β a) and cathode (β b) are applied to describe the polarization behavior of the respective electrodes. These parameters are indicative of the ease with which polarization occurs and the sensitivity of the reaction rate to potential variations. Specifically, a higher βa means that a more substantial shift in anodic potential is necessary to affect a significant alteration in anodic i corr, indicating that the anodic reaction is more difficult to carry out, and thus the anode exhibits greater stability. On the contrary, a lower βc value denotes that even a small adjustment in cathodic potential can cause a considerable change in cathodic i corr, implying that the cathodic reaction proceeds with greater facility. As a result, in the context of metal corrosion protection, a corrosion inhibitor with a higher βa and βc will contribute more to decelerating the corrosion reaction. As shown in Table S1, the nearly parallel configurations of all cathodic branches result in relatively similar βc values, whereas the significant disparity in βa values is due to separations generated in the anodic regions. With the addition of 100 mg L−1, as well as other lower levels, the βa values of RJFME and CDs-JFM are greater, suggesting that these two inhibitors are more excellent. This view is further supported by the higher η P values associated with these two inhibitors across all tested concentrations. Furthermore, according to the previous report [58], the efficacy of a corrosion inhibitor can be elucidated by two action coefficients pertaining to the anodic reaction (f a) and the cathodic reaction (f c), which serve to explain the corrosion/inhibition mechanism.
