Here, the superscript of (inh) and (0) stand for the corrosion reaction occurring in the presence and absence of JFM inhibitors, respectively. Usually, for a given inhibitor, the values of f a and f c are different differ at identical additive concentrations. A diminished value f a either f c causes higher inhibition efficiency [59]. In Table S1, both f a and f c values are <1, indicating that the active sites on CRS surface are covered by JFM inhibitors, which can be due to the geometric locking effect [58]. Besides, the f a or f c values of RJFME and CDs-JFM are lower than those of UJFME at a concentration of 100 mg L−1, corresponding to RJFME and CDs-JFM performing the better electrochemical corrosion inhibition. In addition, both f a and f c decrease with increasing doses of the same JFM inhibitor, accompanied by higher η P values. This further implies that these three JFM inhibitors can be regarded as hybrid inhibitors to resist both anodic and cathodic corrosion reactions simultaneously. Moreover, f a/f
c ratio provides a more intuitive measure to access the relative contribution of anode or cathode inhibition. An intriguing observation from Fig. 7 (c2) is that f a/f c < 1 or very close to 1 at 100 mg L−1 for all three inhibitors, whereas f a/f
c values approach or even exceed 1 at lower levels. This suggests that three JFM inhibitors predominantly tend to exhibit anodic inhibition at higher concentrations but contribute more significantly to cathodic inhibition at lower dosages.

Based on the characteristics of the Nyquist plots, the equivalent circuit (EC) depicted in Fig. 7(b1) is used to fit the EIS data, yielding a small chi-square (χ 2). CE composes of a small uncompensated solution resistant R s and polarization resistance R p, which includes the charge transfer resistance, diffusion layer resistance, membrane resistance, and the resistance related to corrosion products or other species at steel/solution interface [60]. Constant-phase element (CPE) behavior arises from the rough electrode surface, which is attributed to the non-uniform distribution of R s, interfacial capacitance and i corr across the electrode surface. The impedance (Z) of CPE is described by the following Eq. [61].
(10)
Z CPE
=
jω
− n
Y 0
Here, Y 0 and ω represent the magnitude of CPE and the angular frequency, where j = (− 1)1/2 refers to the imaginary number. The parameter n is the phase shift, n < 1 in Table S1 describes the deviation from ideal capacitance behavior (n = 1), which is caused by the uneven steel electrode surface. The related double-layer capacitance (C dl) is subsequently calculated [62].
(11)
C dl
= CPE ×
ω max
n − 1
Here, ω max = 2πf max, f max is the frequency at which the imaginary part reaches its peak. As seen from Fig. 7(c3), C dl value is apparently lower than that of the uninhibited system, and this reduction becomes progressively more pronounced with increasing concentrations of the three JFM inhibitors. This can be explained by the relationship of C dl = ε 0 εA/d [63]. In this equation, A represents the exposed surface area of steel electrode, d is the thickness of the protective film adsorbed by JFM inhibitors on steel electrode, ε 0 and ε stand for the permittivity of the vacuum and the local medium, respectively. Throughout the inhibition process, JFM inhibitor molecules continuously adsorb onto the steel electrode, gradually establishing a compact hydrophobic film that displaces pre-adsorbed H2O molecules from the surface. This action reduces the local dielectric constant and thickens the passive layer, thus resulting in a reduction in C dl and an alleviation of the charge transfer process. As shown in Fig. 7(c4), in comparison to the absolute corrosion in pure HCl medium, R p remarkably increases with rising JFM inhibitor concentration, suggesting progressive densification even thickening of the protective film formed by the JFM inhibitors. In detail, R p values present a consistent hierarchy across all tested concentrations, with CDs-JFM exhibiting the highest resistance, followed by RJFME and then UJFME. This trend is mirrored in the calculated η R. While the η R values of three inhibitors are nearly identical at a concentration of 100 mg L−1 (around 90.0 % ± 1.0). A notable divergence is observed at 10 mg L−1, the η R value of CDs-JFM achieves 84.8 %, far exceeding those of RJFME (55.6 %) and UJFME (48.0 %). This remarkable difference underscores the superior film-forming capacity of CDs-JFM, even at lower dosages. In terms of the Bode modulus, the impedance Z 0.01Hz can be utilized to access the inhibition activity. A greater Z 0.01Hz value leads to better inhibition performance [64]. Compared to the aggressive corrosion in pure HCl solution, the Z 0.01Hz values (Ω cm2) in the inhibited system with 100 mg L−1 JFM inhibitor show the order of CDs-JFM (314.8) > RJFME (270.5) > UJFME (214.0) > blank (30.8). This significant elevation in Z 0.01Hz also confirms the excellent inhibition capacities of these inhibitors, particularly for CDs-JFM. Phase angle (θ) reflects the phase difference between current and voltage in an electrochemical system, offering insights into the electrochemical behavior of electrode surface and the efficacy of protective film. As illustrated in Fig. 7(b2-b4), all θ values consistently remain below 90° in both corrosive and inhibitive environment, indicating a dispersion effect on steel electrode surface [64]. Moreover, the θ values gradually increase with the rising concentrations of JFM inhibitor. This shows that the protective film formed by JFM inhibitors has excellent capacitive properties and effectively prevent the charge transfer process, thus providing robust protection. Notably, the θ values in the presence of CDs-JFM significantly surpass those of the blank HCl solution, and remaining closely aligned across all tested concentrations. This further highlights the exceptional corrosion resistance provided by CDs-JFM, even at a minimal dosage of 10 mg L−1.

Based on the characteristics of the Nyquist plots, the equivalent circuit (EC) depicted in Fig. 7(b1) is used to fit the EIS data, yielding a small chi-square (χ 2). CE composes of a small uncompensated solution resistant R s and polarization resistance R p, which includes the charge transfer resistance, diffusion layer resistance, membrane resistance, and the resistance related to corrosion products or other species at steel/solution interface [60]. Constant-phase element (CPE) behavior arises from the rough electrode surface, which is attributed to the non-uniform distribution of R s, interfacial capacitance and i corr across the electrode surface. The impedance (Z) of CPE is described by the following Eq. [61].
(10)
Z CPE
=
jω
− n
Y 0
Here, Y 0 and ω represent the magnitude of CPE and the angular frequency, where j = (− 1)1/2 refers to the imaginary number. The parameter n is the phase shift, n < 1 in Table S1 describes the deviation from ideal capacitance behavior (n = 1), which is caused by the uneven steel electrode surface. The related double-layer capacitance (C dl) is subsequently calculated [62].
(11)
C dl
= CPE ×
ω max
n − 1
Here, ω max = 2πf max, f max is the frequency at which the imaginary part reaches its peak. As seen from Fig. 7(c3), C dl value is apparently lower than that of the uninhibited system, and this reduction becomes progressively more pronounced with increasing concentrations of the three JFM inhibitors. This can be explained by the relationship of C dl = ε 0 εA/d [63]. In this equation, A represents the exposed surface area of steel electrode, d is the thickness of the protective film adsorbed by JFM inhibitors on steel electrode, ε 0 and ε stand for the permittivity of the vacuum and the local medium, respectively. Throughout the inhibition process, JFM inhibitor molecules continuously adsorb onto the steel electrode, gradually establishing a compact hydrophobic film that displaces pre-adsorbed H2O molecules from the surface. This action reduces the local dielectric constant and thickens the passive layer, thus resulting in a reduction in C dl and an alleviation of the charge transfer process. As shown in Fig. 7(c4), in comparison to the absolute corrosion in pure HCl medium, R p remarkably increases with rising JFM inhibitor concentration, suggesting progressive densification even thickening of the protective film formed by the JFM inhibitors. In detail, R p values present a consistent hierarchy across all tested concentrations, with CDs-JFM exhibiting the highest resistance, followed by RJFME and then UJFME. This trend is mirrored in the calculated η R. While the η R values of three inhibitors are nearly identical at a concentration of 100 mg L−1 (around 90.0 % ± 1.0). A notable divergence is observed at 10 mg L−1, the η R value of CDs-JFM achieves 84.8 %, far exceeding those of RJFME (55.6 %) and UJFME (48.0 %). This remarkable difference underscores the superior film-forming capacity of CDs-JFM, even at lower dosages. In terms of the Bode modulus, the impedance Z 0.01Hz can be utilized to access the inhibition activity. A greater Z 0.01Hz value leads to better inhibition performance [64]. Compared to the aggressive corrosion in pure HCl solution, the Z 0.01Hz values (Ω cm2) in the inhibited system with 100 mg L−1 JFM inhibitor show the order of CDs-JFM (314.8) > RJFME (270.5) > UJFME (214.0) > blank (30.8). This significant elevation in Z 0.01Hz also confirms the excellent inhibition capacities of these inhibitors, particularly for CDs-JFM. Phase angle (θ) reflects the phase difference between current and voltage in an electrochemical system, offering insights into the electrochemical behavior of electrode surface and the efficacy of protective film. As illustrated in Fig. 7(b2-b4), all θ values consistently remain below 90° in both corrosive and inhibitive environment, indicating a dispersion effect on steel electrode surface [64]. Moreover, the θ values gradually increase with the rising concentrations of JFM inhibitor. This shows that the protective film formed by JFM inhibitors has excellent capacitive properties and effectively prevent the charge transfer process, thus providing robust protection. Notably, the θ values in the presence of CDs-JFM significantly surpass those of the blank HCl solution, and remaining closely aligned across all tested concentrations. This further highlights the exceptional corrosion resistance provided by CDs-JFM, even at a minimal dosage of 10 mg L−1.

Fig. 8 displays a sequence of surface morphologies observed on CRS samples following a 6 h immersion in HCl medium at 303 K, both in the absence and presence of three distinct JFM inhibitors. AFM stands as a formidable tool for detecting the surface roughness of metals from the micrometer and even nanometer scale. In Fig. 8(a1), due to the aggressive attack of Cl−, the corrosive CRS surface is extremely uneven with an average roughness (R a) reaching 157 nm. However, with the addition of 100 mg L−1 JFM inhibitors, the 3D and 2D-AFM images shown in Fig. 8(a2-a4) become flatter than those in blank HCl solution. The section lines demonstrate less fluctuations, and the R a values are sharply reduced to around 50 nm. 3D-LM maps displayed in Fig. 8(b), a notable contrast is seen between the uninhibited and inhibited CRS samples. While the corroded sample exhibits large amplitude variations, the height of inhibited CRS surface folds back towards the zero-reference plane. This is accompanied by a reduction in arithmetic mean height (S a) values. As evidenced by the SEM (Fig. 8(c)) and MM (Fig. 8(e)) images, the corroded CRS surface exhibits a highly rough texture, characterized by numerous pits and an abundance of corrosion products. In stark contrast, the introduction of JFM inhibitors markedly alters this scenario. The once prevalent surface irregularities vanish, giving way to a lustrous finish. The inhibitors' efficacy is further highlighted by the visibility of pre-existing scratches and the exposure of the underlying steel substrate, standing out the protective qualities of JFM inhibitors. In EDS mapping (Fig. 8(d)) analysis, a significant presence of Fe is observed alongside a variety of organic elements, with their quantitative percentages detailed in Fig. 8(c). The highest content of O (3.74 %) is identified on corroded sample, a consequence of the production of Fe oxides on CRS surface, which promotes the corrosion reaction [65]. Conversely, the decrease in O content post-inhibition may be attributed to the dissolution rate of these oxides in acidic environment surpasses the adsorption rate of O-containing compounds from the JFM inhibitors. The appearance of N and S further prove the adsorption activity of JFM inhibitors, indicating their role in mitigating corrosion. Regarding Cl element, only 0.01 % is detected in corroded sample, while none is found in JFM-inhibited samples. Although pitting corrosion by Cl− is thermodynamically inevitable in HCl environment, this observation still confirms that CRS corrosion in inhibited system is significantly mitigated compared to blank HCl solution. WCA serves as a critical metric for assessing the wettability of a solid surface by a liquid, which is associated with the surface's chemical constituents and microstructure. Upon immersion in a pure HCl solution, the CRS surface exhibits pronounced hydrophilicity, as indicated by a low WCA of 29.7°, signaling substantial corrosion and the presence of corrosive pits. In comparison, the addition of 100 mg L−1 JFM inhibitors leads to a notable increase in WCA values to approximately 80.0°, denoting a transition towards hydrophobicity on CRS surface. This shift implies the inhibitors' effectiveness in altering the CRS surface properties to resist corrosion. These observed changes in CRS surface morphology, roughness, elemental composition, and wettability collectively corroborate the ability of three JFM inhibitors to form a protective barrier, thereby significantly enhancing the corrosion resistance in HCl medium.

XPS and TOF-SIMS analysis are applied to obtain the bonding information of CRS sample immersed in 1.0 M HCl solution containing 100 mg L−1 of JFM inhibitors for a duration of 6 h. As can be seen from the full XPS spectrum in Fig. 9 , distinct peaks corresponding to C, O, N, S and Fe are detected. Despite the relatively low intensities of the N and S signals, unequivocally indicates the adsorption of N- and S-associated groups onto the CRS surface. In Fe 2p spectrum, the peak at 706.1 eV corresponds to Fe0, which represents unreactive Fe [66]. The relatively smaller proportion of Fe0 in the CRS inhibited by UJFME aligns with the poorer performance in PDP and EIS plots upon the addition of UJFME. The peaks at 710.2 eV and 724.8 eV are assigned to Fe (II) 2p3/2 and Fe (II) 2p1/2, respectively, while the peaks at 713.3 eV and 725.5 eV are attributed to Fe (III) 2p3/2 and Fe (III) 2p1/2 [67]. These peaks mainly arise from corrosive products and the binding of Fe with the O or N from the JFM inhibitors, like Fe3+, Fe2+, FeO or FeN [50]. C 1 s is involved into three diffractive peaks of CC/CH, CO/CN and C=O/OCO bonds, located at 284.8 eV, 286.4–287.2 eV and 288.8 eV respectively [50]. Evidently, the two peaks at 292.6 and 295.4 eV are not found on CDs-JFM-treated sample. This confirms the conjugated C-structure in CDs-JFM, for example CC bonds and benzene ring are capable of interacting with CRS surface, resulting in the loss of its characteristic features. N 1 s spectrum shows two peaks: one at 404.3 eV for CN and another in the range of 398.9–400.2 eV for NH/FeN bonds [68]. Three fitted peaks in O 1 s correspond to FeO/FeOOH at 529.8 eV, CO at 531.3 eV and adsorbed water molecules H2Oads at 532.9 eV [69,70]. The intensity of S 2p is really weak, the peaks for S2p1/2 and S2p3/2 can be categorized as follows: S2− or FeS complex at 161.5 eV, S attached to C like SC at 163.6 eV, S in oxidized forms such as SO at 166.3 eV and SO at 168.3 eV [33,71]. These findings provide insights into the chemical interactions and bonding states of Fe-, C-, O-, N- and S-species on CRS surface. They offer powerful evidence that the organic components from three JFM inhibitors can interact with CRS surface through chemical binding, such as the formation of FeO, FeN, FeS, and other complexes. This confirms the ability of three inhibitors to chemically adsorb onto CRS surface, playing a key role in their corrosion inhibition mechanisms.

To further determine the adsorbed film and the adsorption type of JFM inhibitors, TOF-SIMS analysis was carried out. As shown in Fig. 10 , the fragment information of three inhibited CRS surfaces exhibits general similarities. Specifically, C-, O- and N-containing fragments are the characteristics of JFM inhibitors, while Fe oxides and hydroxides primarily correspond to corrosion products. Additionally, the bindings between Fe and organic atoms or groups are associated with adsorption species formed via chemical bonds. For instance, residues of CN (m/z 26), C2H3 (m/z 27), C2H5 (m/z 29), CNO (m/z 42), C2H3O (m/z 43) and C6H5 (m/z 77) are derived from the breakage or cleavage of based C-structures in JFM inhibitors [72]. The fragments of Fe oxide/hydroxide like FeO (m/z 72), FeOH (m/z 73) and FeO2H (m/z 89) are considered as oxidation products resulting from corrosion reaction. Importantly, FeC x H x and FeO x C x H x fragments are related to a weakly bonded layer, suggesting the occurring of chemical interactions between Fe and organic species [73]. A notable appearance of FeN (m/z 70) on the CRS surface inhibited by CDs-JFM, which is not be prominently detected in the other two inhibited specimens. This infers that a greater number of N- sites might have been exposed and activated under the high temperature and pressure conditions of HTS process. From the TOF-SIMS mappings, the warm color represents the stronger signals, revealing that the adsorption strength follows the turn: CDs-JFM > RJFME > UJFME. This order is also corresponded in the intensities of two identical debris, FeC2H and FeC3H2. These observations highlight the unique chemical interactions enabled by CDs-JFM, potentially contributing to its enhanced corrosion inhibition performance. In summary, the combined data from XPS and TOF-SIMS provide further proofs of the chemical interactions between the JFM inhibitors and CRS surface, supporting the proposed adsorption mechanism.

Owing to the excellent fluorescent properties of CDs-JFM, LSCM was employed to observe the intensity and distribution of fluorescent signals on inhibited CRS surface, thus providing valuable insights into the adsorption behavior and spatial distribution of this inhibitor. Fig. 11(a) shows the LSCM diagram of CRS immersed in 1.0 M HCl solution with 100 mg L−1 CDs-JFM at various point in reaction time. Initially (0 h), no fluorescent signal is detected on the pure corrosive CRS surface in the absence of CDs-JFM. After 0.5 h of immersion, fluorescent signals begin to appear on CRS surface, suggesting that the adsorption of CDs-JFM may occur more slowly compared to conventional organic molecules. As the immersion time increases, the fluorescent signals become progressively more pronounced. By 6 h, the fluorescent signals nearly cover the entire CRS surface, particularly observed by 3D-LSCM. This specific adsorption behavior of CDs-JFM can be ascribed to the interactions between CRS surface and the abundant heteroatoms (O, N, S), as well as unsaturated C-groups within CDs-JFM. These results complement the XPS and TOF-SIMS findings, providing visual confirmation of the CDs-JFM film-formation mechanism responsible for its superior inhibition performance.

Electrochemical corrosion of metals in acidic environments predominantly involves two fundamental processes: the reduction of hydrogen ions and the oxidation of metal. In this study, the corrosion of steel in HCl medium can be simply interpreted as Fe → Fe2+ + 2e− at anode and 2H3O+ + 2e− → H2 + 2H2O at cathode.

In order to better understand the corrosion behavior, it is crucial to determine the electrification state (positive or negative) of steel surface. This can be achieved using Antropov's potential (E A), which is the potential difference (ΔE diff) between the steel surface and the surrounding solution when the steel/solution interface reaches equilibrium. Actually, E A reflects the intrinsic tendency or ability of the steel to either gain or lose electrons when immersed in electrolyte solution. This parameter is closely linked to the dissolution and ionization processes of the steel, providing critical insights into its corrosion dynamics. As illustrated in Fig. 11(b), the R p-E plots depict the variation in R p of steel electrode in both 1.0 M pure HCl and that containing 100 mg L−1 of JFM inhibitors. The E value corresponding to the maximum R p value stands for E PZC. E A can be calculated through the equation of E A = E OCP – E PZC [54]. In this study, E A < 0 in all reaction systems indicates that the steel surface has a lower potential relative to the HCl solution at steel/HCl solution interface. This phenomenon can be illustrated from the following aspects. Firstly, the oxidative species such as H3O+ are more inclined to capture electrons from Fe surface, leading to the generation of H2 and making the steel surface to accumulate excess anions like Cl−, thereby becoming negatively charged [20,74]. Secondly, the existence of the double-layer structure creates a certain ΔE diff between the steel surface and the interior of HCl solution. Steel has a stronger tendency to dissolve in the HCl medium, resulting in a relatively lower potential at steel surface. Thirdly, this behavior can be further explained by the Nernst equation [75], which provides a thermodynamic basis for understanding the potential distribution at the interface.
(12)
E =
E 0
+
RT xF
ln
Fe
2 +
Fe
Here, x is the number of electrons transferred in electrode reaction, F donates the Faraday constant. Due to the extremely low initial concentration of Fe2+ in HCl solution, the electrode potential E to move towards the negative direction relative to the standard electrode potential E 0, consequently resulting in a negative value for E A. Furthermore, the E A value in pure corrosive HCl media is measured at −30 mV, whereas in the presence of RJFME, UJFME and CDs-JFM inhibitors, the E A values shift to −10 mV, −20 mV and − 20 mV, respectively. These positive shifts in E A illustrate several key points. Firstly, the ΔE diff between steel and HCl medium is decreased, thus diminishing the driving force for corrosion reaction. This reduction subsequently slows down the corrosion rate and lowers i corr value, effectively inhibiting the cathodic reaction. Another is that the steel surface becomes less susceptible to electron loss and exhibits lower electrochemical active, which retards the anodic reaction.

As depicted in Fig. 12 , both RJFME and UJFME contain substantial organic compounds, which cover functional groups such as CO, CC, benzene ring and N, S heteroatoms. These mentioned atoms and groups are also distributed on the CDs-JFM surface. These sites, which are either electron-rich or possess unbonded orbitals, could spontaneously interact with the steel surface through two distinct mechanisms. One mechanism involves electrostatic adsorption and van der Waals forces between the positively charged protonated atoms in the inhibitors and the negatively charged steel surface covered with excess Cl−. This interaction, known as physisorption, effectively locks the Cl−, thereby inhibiting their ability to initiate pitting corrosion on steel surface. The second mechanism engages the transfer of surplus electrons from the steel surface to the unoccupied orbitals of the inhibitor molecules. Simultaneously, the lone pair electrons of heteroatoms such as O, N and S, as well as the π-electrons of functional groups like CC bonds and benzene ring within inhibitor molecules, can be donate to the vacant 3d-orbital of Fe atom. This electron exchange facilitates the formation of covalent bonds such as OFe, NFe and SFe, or chelation complexes, a process is referred to as chemisorption. This strong chemical interaction further enhances the protective effect of JFM inhibitors on steel surface. These combined effects collectively contribute to the formation of a protective JFM film, which effectively impedes the movement of aggressive species such as Cl−, H3O+ and H2O towards steel surface. Additionally, the superior inhibition performance of CDs-JFM can be attributed to several factors. First, owing to its nanoscale dimensions, CDs-JFM can undergo self-aggregation and deposit spontaneously onto steel surface, forming a protective film. Second, CDs-JFM typically exhibit larger molecular dimensions (nanometer-scale) compared to conventional organic inhibitors (angstrom-scale) derived from the two JFM extracts. Consequently, the adsorbed film formed by CDs-JFM is thicker and denser, providing a more effective shielding effect. Overall, by establishing a robust barrier that restricts the access of corrosive species, the JFM film significantly enhances the corrosion resistance of steel, ensuring long-term durability even in harsh environments.

In summary, this work addresses the corrosion inhibition requirements of CRS in HCl pickling process. Three novel and highly effective inhibitors, RJFME, UJFME and CDs-JFM are developed for the first time, from the Jatropha curcas fruit seeds meal (JFM). Their excellent inhibition efficiencies are achieved 96.5 %, 93.5 % and 96.2 %, respectively, with the addition of 100 mg L−1. This dosage is lower than those reported in existing studies, yet it achieves comparable efficacy. The distribution of active sites including CO, CC, benzene ring and N, S heteroatoms confers their corrosion inhibition potential. Functioning as mixed-type inhibitors, RJFME, UJFME and CDs-JFM at 100 mg L−1 demonstrate significant corrosion inhibition effects, suppressing the corrosion current density from 884 μA cm−2 to 31, 70, and 34 μA cm−2, respectively, while increasing the polarization resistance from 23.0 Ω cm2 to 266.2, 204.8, and 309.6 Ω cm2. Particularly, CDs-JFM exhibits outstanding performance, showing enhanced passivation stability. Additionally, they form protective films on CRS surface by Langmuir adsorption mode, dramatically blocking the diffusion of corrosive particles of Cl−, H3O+ and H2O. Surface analysis reveals that CRS samples inhibited by these inhibitors exhibit similar roughness and hydrophobicity, further confirming their desirable inhibition capacities. Notably, the unique fluorescence properties of CDs-JFM provide insights into its inhibition mechanism. In terms of preparation, UJFME is the least time-consuming. CDs-JFM is the simplest to operate and most water-soluble, albeit requiring high energy consumption and longer reaction time. These two inhibitors demonstrate strong resistance to the highly corrosive HCl environments. However, UJFME exhibits relatively weak adsorption at all studied temperatures and the insufficient inhibition efficiency in electrochemical tests. Reflux requires longer reaction time than ultrasound-assistance, but operates at lower temperatures compared to hydrothermal synthesis. RJFME shows moderate performance across various assessments. Comprehensively, CDs-JFM stands out as a more superior corrosion inhibitor, offering great potential for further development and application. This work not only highlights the inhibitory potential of a sustainable JFM, but also underscores the importance of preparation methods in enhancing its inhibition performance. Looking ahead, our primary focus will be to explore and refine the preparation techniques of JFM inhibitors, particularly further emphasis on optimizing the synthesis conditions of CDs-JFM to produce nanomaterials with smaller dimensions and superior properties. Additionally, we will advance the compositional analysis and structural characterization of both JFM extracts and CDs-JFM, aiming to achieve a more precise and comprehensive understanding of their inhibition mechanisms.
